exercises.title: Cache hit vs miss — DB calls counted
exercises.scenario: @Cacheable skips the method body on cache hits.
exercises.expected_solution:
DB HIT prints 2 times
Call 1: cache miss → DB HIT
Calls 2,3: cache hits → no DB HIT
Cache cleared
Call 4: cache miss → DB HIT
Call 5: cache hit → no DB HIT

test_cases.expected:
DB HIT prints 2 times
Call 1: cache miss → DB HIT
Calls 2,3: cache hits → no DB HIT
Cache cleared
Call 4: cache miss → DB HIT
Call 5: cache hit → no DB HIT

hints: First call: MISS → method runs → result cached
Same key: HIT → method skipped
After clear: next call is a MISS again

exercises.title: Add @Cacheable and @CacheEvict
exercises.scenario: Read methods get @Cacheable; write methods get @CacheEvict.

blanks.hint: Activates Spring caching — put on Application class

hints: @EnableCaching: required to activate caching support
@Cacheable on reads, @CacheEvict on writes

exercises.title: Minimal @Cacheable service
exercises.scenario: Add @Cacheable to a country lookup service.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `CountryService` with @EnableCaching + @Cacheable:
1. findByCode(String code) → caches each country by code
2. findAll() → caches the full list
3. save(String code, String name) → evicts all entries

Print 'DB HIT: {method}' inside each DB method.
hints: @Cacheable(value='countries', key='#code'): key = the code parameter
@Cacheable('countries') for no-arg: key = SimpleKey.EMPTY
@CacheEvict(allEntries=true): clears ALL entries in the cache

exercises.title: @CachePut — update cache on write
exercises.scenario: @CachePut always executes the method AND updates the cache.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `UserService` with:
1. `getUser(String id)` → @Cacheable by id
2. `updateUser(User user)` → @CachePut (updates cache with new value after DB write)
3. `deleteUser(String id)` → @CacheEvict by id

Show the difference: after @CachePut, the next getUser() is a cache HIT with the new data.
hints: @CachePut: method always runs (unlike @Cacheable which skips on hit)
key='#result.id': use the RETURN VALUE's id as cache key
After @CachePut, next getUser(id) returns updated cached value

exercises.title: Caffeine cache configuration
exercises.scenario: Caffeine provides TTL, max size, and statistics.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `CacheConfig` @Configuration that creates a Caffeine-backed CacheManager with:
- 'products' cache: max 1000, TTL 5 minutes, record stats
- 'prices' cache: max 200, TTL 30 seconds, record stats
hints: Caffeine.newBuilder().maximumSize(N).expireAfterWrite(Duration).recordStats().build()
manager.registerCustomCache('name', caffeineCache)
recordStats(): enables hit rate monitoring

exercises.title: Cache key expressions — SpEL
exercises.scenario: SpEL expressions create unique cache keys from parameters.
exercises.instructions:
Write four @Cacheable methods with these key expressions:
1. findByUserId(String userId) → key = userId
2. findByUserAndType(String userId, String type) → key = userId + ':' + type
3. findTop(int limit) → key = 'top:' + limit
4. findByDateRange(LocalDate from, LocalDate to) → key = from + '_' + to
hints: #paramName: references a method parameter in SpEL
'literal' + #param: string concatenation in SpEL (note single quotes for string literal)
Key must be unique across methods — use prefixes to avoid collision

exercises.title: @SpringBootTest — verify cache hit
exercises.scenario: Assert the DB is only hit once across multiple identical calls.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test that:
1. Clears the cache in @BeforeEach
2. Calls countryService.findAll() twice
3. Verifies both results are equal
4. Verifies the 'countries' cache has an entry for SimpleKey.EMPTY
5. After clearing cache, calls once more and verifies a fresh entry
hints: @BeforeEach cache clear: essential for test isolation
SimpleKey.EMPTY: default key for no-argument @Cacheable methods
cache.get(key) returns null if not cached

exercises.title: Conditional caching — condition and unless
exercises.scenario: Cache only when conditions are met.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `SearchService` with conditional caching:
1. search(String query): cache only if query length >= 3 (condition)
2. findTop(int n): cache unless n > 100 (unless — very large n not worth caching)
3. findByPriceRange(double min, double max): cache only if results are non-empty
hints: condition: evaluated BEFORE method runs; can use #paramName
unless: evaluated AFTER method runs; can use #result
#query.length(): SpEL method call on parameter

exercises.title: @Caching — multiple operations in one method
exercises.instructions:
Write `ProductService.delete(String id)` using @Caching to:
1. Evict the product's individual cache entry (key=id)
2. Evict the entire 'products' list cache (allEntries=true)
3. Evict the product from the 'products-by-category' cache (allEntries=true)

Also write `ProductService.updateProduct(Product p)` using @Caching to:
- Evict list cache
- Put updated product in product cache
hints: @Caching: combines multiple @CacheEvict, @CachePut, @Cacheable
evict = {@CacheEvict(...), @CacheEvict(...)}: multiple evictions
put + evict: update individual entry AND clear list cache

exercises.title: @CachePut vs @CacheEvict — what happens next
exercises.scenario: CachePut updates; CacheEvict removes; next read differs.
exercises.instructions:
After each operation, is the next getUser('U01') a cache HIT or MISS, and what does it return?

Start: getUser('U01') → 'Aisha' (cached)

Scenario A: `@CachePut updateUser(new User('U01','Aisha-Updated','...'))` called
→ next getUser('U01') = ?

Scenario B: `@CacheEvict deleteUser('U01')` called
→ next getUser('U01') = ?
exercises.expected_solution:
Scenario A: HIT — returns 'Aisha-Updated' (CachePut updated the cache entry)
Scenario B: MISS — DB query runs, returns current DB value (cache entry was removed)

test_cases.expected:
Scenario A: HIT — returns 'Aisha-Updated' (CachePut updated the cache entry)
Scenario B: MISS — DB query runs, returns current DB value (cache entry was removed)

hints: @CachePut: always runs the method AND stores the result in cache
@CacheEvict: removes the cache entry entirely
After @CachePut: next read is a HIT with the PUT value
After @CacheEvict: next read is a MISS → DB query

exercises.title: Stock price cache — Zerodha style
exercises.scenario: Stock prices need short TTL; company info needs long TTL.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `StockService` with:
- `getPrice(String symbol)` → @Cacheable('prices'), key=symbol — 30s TTL
- `getCompanyInfo(String symbol)` → @Cacheable('companies'), key=symbol — 1h TTL
- `updatePrice(String symbol, double price)` → @CachePut('prices'), key=symbol
- Configure CacheManager with both Caffeine caches and correct TTLs
hints: Two caches with different TTLs: prices=30s, companies=1h
@CachePut for price updates: market data changes frequently
recordStats() for hit rate monitoring in production

exercises.title: Verify cache with Mockito Spy
exercises.scenario: Spy on the real service to count actual DB calls.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test using Mockito.spy() on ProductRepository:
1. Create spy of the repo
2. Call productService.findAll() twice
3. Verify repo.findAll() was called exactly ONCE (second call was cached)
4. Call productService.save(product)
5. Call productService.findAll() again
6. Verify repo.findAll() was called again after eviction
hints: Check cache.get(SimpleKey.EMPTY) for presence/absence
@CacheEvict on save: cache cleared after save
Next findAll() after eviction: cache miss → fresh DB hit

exercises.title: Full product catalog with caching — CRUD
exercises.scenario: Complete cached product service with proper eviction strategy.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete `ProductCatalogService` with caching:
- findAll() → @Cacheable, TTL 5min
- findById(id) → @Cacheable per-id, TTL 5min
- findByCategory(cat) → @Cacheable per-category, TTL 5min
- save(product) → @CachePut (product entry) + @CacheEvict (list caches)
- update(id, product) → @CachePut product + @CacheEvict lists
- delete(id) → @Caching evict all three

@SpringBootTest with 4 tests verifying cache hits and evictions.
hints: @Caching with both put and evict: update individual + clear list
SimpleKey.EMPTY: key for findAll() (no args)
@BeforeEach clears cache AND reseeds data

exercises.title: application.properties Caffeine spec
exercises.scenario: Configure Caffeine via properties without a @Bean.
exercises.instructions:
Show the application.properties configuration equivalent to:
- Cache type: Caffeine
- Cache names: products, prices, users
- Global spec: max 1000, expire after write 5 minutes
- Then override prices with 30-second TTL in a @Bean

Write the properties file AND the @Bean override for prices only.
hints: spring.cache.caffeine.spec: Caffeine spec string (uses Caffeine's own format)
expireAfterWrite=5m: 5 minutes in Caffeine spec notation
registerCustomCache() overrides the global spec for a specific cache

exercises.title: Complete product API with cache — Module 14.3 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Production-ready Spring Boot service with Caffeine caching.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete cached Razorpay-style merchant configuration service:
- `MerchantConfig` @Entity (merchantId, webhookUrl, maxAmount, currency, enabled)
- `MerchantConfigService` with @Cacheable per-merchantId (TTL 10min), @CachePut on update, @CacheEvict on delete
- `CacheConfig` with Caffeine: merchant-config cache (max=5000, 10min TTL, recordStats)
- @SpringBootTest tests: cache hit verification, eviction on update, eviction on delete, fresh cache after TTL simulation
- All 4 tests pass

This is the exact pattern used for merchant config in payment gateways.
hints: Cache key = merchantId (String primary key)
@CachePut key='#result.merchantId': use the returned entity's id
@BeforeEach: clear cache AND reload test data
